cards.definition: A persistent body of documents kept outside the model, holding facts the model was never trained on or cannot reliably recall.
cards.movie_element_detail: Leonard Shelby cannot form new long-term memories, so he keeps everything he must not lose outside his head: annotated Polaroid photos, handwritten notes, and tattoos reserved for the facts that matter most. The archive persists perfectly while his mind retains nothing.
cards.mapping_line: Leonard can't hold new memories, so he keeps what he must know on photos, notes, and skin.
cards.break_point: Leonard's archive holds only what one man could write; real systems store millions of documents no single author ever curated.
cards.bridge: The store is a database of documents kept outside the model, searched at question time instead of memorized during training.

cards.definition: Splitting documents into small self-contained pieces sized for retrieval and for fitting into the model's limited input.
cards.movie_element: The numbered fact tattoos and one-caption Polaroids
cards.movie_element_detail: Leonard's knowledge is broken into small self-contained units: numbered 'Fact' tattoos and photos carrying a single handwritten caption. Each unit must stand alone, because whoever reads it next — future Leonard — arrives with no surrounding context and only a short window to act.
cards.mapping_line: Every fact gets its own tattoo or photo caption, small enough to grasp before his memory resets.
cards.break_point: Leonard writes each note as a complete thought; automated splitting is mechanical and can cut an idea in half at the seams.
cards.bridge: Documents are split into passage-sized pieces so the search can return just the relevant fragment, not a whole book.

cards.definition: Finding the stored pieces most relevant to the current question, typically by comparing meaning rather than exact words.
cards.movie_element: Leonard checking his Polaroids to identify who he's facing
cards.movie_element_detail: When Leonard meets someone, he flips through his Polaroids until he finds their photo and reads the caption — pulling the one stored record relevant to the situation in front of him, then acting on it.
cards.mapping_line: Faced with a stranger, Leonard flips through his photos and pulls the one that matches.
cards.break_point: Leonard matches faces by eye; real search matches meaning mathematically, ranking thousands of candidates in milliseconds without anyone looking.
cards.bridge: A search engine compares the question's meaning against every stored piece and returns the closest matches automatically.

cards.definition: Placing retrieved text into the model's input for one request, so it can use facts it never memorized.
cards.movie_element: 'Don't believe his lies' guiding Leonard's next move
cards.movie_element_detail: The caption on Teddy's Polaroid — 'Don't believe his lies' — enters Leonard's awareness the moment he reads it and steers everything he does, but only until the next reset, when he must pull the photo out and read it again.
cards.mapping_line: The note works only while he's reading it — next reset, he must pull it out again.
cards.break_point: Leonard obeys his notes absolutely; a model treats added text as one input it can weigh, doubt, or ignore.
cards.bridge: Retrieved passages are pasted into the prompt beside the question; the model reads them fresh on every single request.

cards.definition: Tracking which document each retrieved fact came from, so answers can be attributed and verified.
cards.movie_element: Leonard tattooing a false 'fact' for his future self
cards.movie_element_detail: Leonard trusts records because of their origin — his own handwriting, ink on his own skin. In the ending, after Teddy tells him the truth, Leonard deliberately writes down Teddy's license plate as a new fact, planting a false record his future self will treat as verified evidence.
cards.mapping_line: Future Leonard trusts the tattoo completely because he can't ask who wrote it down, or why.
cards.break_point: Real systems can show the original document for readers to check; Leonard has no way to re-verify where a note came from.
cards.bridge: Answers cite which document each claim came from, so a human can open the source and judge its reliability.
